blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (١)
The Besieger! (1)
مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٢)
What is the Besieger?! (2)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ (٣)
What will show you what is the Besieger?! (3)
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعَادٌۢ بِٱلْقَارِعَةِ (٤)
Thamudand ‘Ad denied the Cataclysm. (4)
فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِٱلطَّاغِيَةِ (٥)
As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the Cry. (5)
وَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ (٦)
And as for ‘Ad, they were destroyed by a fierce icy gale, (6)
سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا فَتَرَى ٱلْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ (٧)
which He clamped on them for seven gruelling nights and eight days, so that you could see the people there lying about prostrate as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees. (7)
فَهَلْ تَرَىٰ لَهُم مِّنۢ بَاقِيَةٍ (٨)
So do you see any remaining trace of them? (8)
وَجَآءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَن قَبْلَهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتُ بِٱلْخَاطِئَةِ (٩)
Then Pharaoh and those who were before him, and the towns that were overturned, brought about iniquity. (9)
فَعَصَوْا۟ رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رَّابِيَةً (١٠)
They disobeyed the apostle of their Lord, so He seized them with a terrible seizing. (10)
إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَا ٱلْمَآءُ حَمَلْنَـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْجَارِيَةِ (١١)
Indeed when the Flood rose high, We carried you in a floating ark, (11)
لِنَجْعَلَهَا لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَهَآ أُذُنٌ وَٰعِيَةٌ (١٢)
that We might make it a reminder for you, and that receptive ears might remember it. (12)

(Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Inevitable (الحاقة) — the Great Resurrection — and has also referred to it as the Clatterer (القارعة) and the Imminent Event (الواقعة).

The discourse within it is organized into three sections: a section mentioning in brief the nations who denied it, whereupon Allah seized them with a surpassing punishment; a section describing the Inevitable and the division of people therein into the People of the Right and the People of the Left, along with the difference in their states of felicity and wretchedness; and a section emphasizing the truthfulness of the Quran in its foretelling of it, and that it is the Certain Truth (حق اليقين). The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ — The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable? And what will show you what the Inevitable is? (1-3). What is meant by the Inevitable (الحاقة) is the Great Resurrection. It is named thus because of its established certainty (ثبوتها), a reality that cannot be averted and in which there is no doubt; derived from the "truth" (حق) of a thing, meaning it is fixed and established with an actual reality (تقررا واقعيا).

The word "What" (مَا) in "What is the Inevitable?" (مَا الْحَاقَّةُ) is interrogative, serving to magnify its matter (تفخيم أمرها). For this very reason, the explicit noun was used in place of a pronoun; He did not say, "What is it?" The interrogative sentence serves as the predicate (خبر) for "The Inevitable" (الحاقة).

Thus, His saying: "The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable?" is intended to magnify the matter of the Resurrection, conveying its gravity and the greatness of its reality with repeated emphasis.

His saying: "And what will show you what the Inevitable is?" is an address negating the [ordinary] knowledge of the reality of that Day. This expression is an allusion (كناية) to the absolute importance of the matter and its reaching the ultimate degree of grandeur. Perhaps this is what is meant by what was narrated from Ibn Abbas: "Whatever occurs in the Quran of His saying, the Exalted: 'What will show you' (ما أَدْراكَ), He has indeed shown him; and whatever occurs of His saying: 'What will make you know' (ما يُدْرِيكَ), He has kept it hidden from him." This means that "What will show you" is an allusion, while "What will make you know" is an explicit statement.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعادٌ بِالْقارِعَةِ — Thamud and ‘Ad denied the Clatterer. (4). What is meant by the Clatterer (القارعة) is the Resurrection. It is named thus because it strikes (تقرع) and crushes the heavens and the earth by changing them, the mountains by setting them in motion, the sun by folding it up, the moon by eclipsing it, the stars by scattering them, and all things by its overwhelming power, as stated in the verses. The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "Thamud and ‘Ad denied it," but the "Clatterer" was placed in the position of the pronoun to emphasize the magnification of its matter.

This verse and those following it until the completion of nine verses, although intended to point briefly to the stories of the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, Pharaoh, those before him, and the overturned towns, and their destruction, are in reality an exposition of the Inevitable (الحاقة) through some of its attributes—namely, that Allah destroyed many nations for denying it. Thus, it is effectively an answer to the question posed by the interrogative "What," just as His saying: "Then when the Trumpet is blown..." etc., is another answer.

The summary of the meaning is: It is the Clatterer which was denied by Thamud, ‘Ad, Pharaoh, those before him, the overturned towns, and the people of Noah; so Allah seized them with a surpassing seizure and destroyed them with an extirpating punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ — As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the Overwhelming [Cry]. (5). This is a detailed exposition of the consequence of their denial of the Clatterer.

What is meant by the Overwhelming (الطَّاغِيَةِ) is the Cry (الصيحة), the Earthquake (الرجفة), or the Thunderbolt (الصاعقة), according to the different outward expressions of the Quran regarding the cause of their destruction in their story. The Exalted said: وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ — "The Cry seized those who were wrongdoing" (Hud: 67); and He also said: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ — "So the earthquake seized them" (Al-A'raf: 87); and He also said: فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صاعِقَةُ الْعَذابِ الْهُونِ — "The thunderbolt of a humiliating punishment seized them" (Fussilat: 17).

It is also said that the Overwhelming (الطاغية) is a verbal noun (مصدر) like "transgression" (الطغيان) or "rebellion" (الطغوى), and the meaning would be: "As for Thamud, they were destroyed because of their transgression." This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْواها — "Thamud denied [the truth] because of their rebellion" (Ash-Shams: 11).

However, the first of the two views is more appropriate to the context of the subsequent verses, as they are presented to explain the manner of their destruction—whether by the wind, the surpassing seizure, or the transgression of the water. Therefore, the destruction of Thamud by the "Overwhelming" should refer to the manner of their destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا عادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عاتِيَةٍ — And as for ‘Ad, they were destroyed by a fierce, icy gale. (6). The "fierce gale" (الصرصر) is a cold wind with a powerful blast, and "icy" (عاتية) is from "insolence" (العتو), meaning transgression and departure from obedience and suitability.

His saying, the Exalted: سَخَّرَها عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيالٍ وَثَمانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُوماً فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيها صَرْعى كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَةٍ — which He let loose on them for seven nights and eight days consecutively, so that you could see the people therein lying prostrate as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees. (7). Letting it loose (تسخيرها) upon them means giving it power over them. "Consecutively" (الحسوم) is the plural of "decisive" (حاسم), like "witnesses" (شهود) is the plural of "witness" (شاهد), derived from "severing" (الحسم) in the sense of repeating cauterization several times in succession. It is an adjective for the "seven," meaning seven nights and eight days in continuous succession. "Prostrate" (صرعى) is the plural of "fallen" (صريع). "Trunks" (أعجاز) is the plural of "trunk" (عجز), the end of a thing. "Hollow" (خاوية) means empty inside and cast down. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَهَلْ تَرى لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَةٍ — Do you see any of them remaining? (8). That is, any soul remaining. The sentence is an allusion to the total destruction that encompassed them all. It is also said that "remaining" (الباقية) is a verbal noun meaning "survival" (البقاء), intended here as "remnant" (البقية). The meaning we previously mentioned is closer.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَنْ قَبْلَهُ وَالْمُؤْتَفِكاتُ بِالْخاطِئَةِ — Pharaoh and those who were before him and the overturned towns committed sins. (9). What is meant by Pharaoh is the Pharaoh of Moses; by "those before him," the nations that preceded him in time among the deniers; and by "the overturned towns" (المؤتفكات), the villages of the people of Lot and the community residing therein. "Sin" (خاطئة) is a verbal noun meaning "error" (الخطاء), and "committing sin" refers to erring from the path of servitude (العبودية). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَعَصَوْا رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رابِيَةً — And they disobeyed the apostle of their Lord, so He seized them with a surpassing seizure. (10). The pronoun in "they disobeyed" (فَعَصَوْا) refers to Pharaoh, those before him, and the overturned towns. What is meant by "the apostle" is the genus. "Surpassing" (الرابية) means increasing, from "it increased" (ربا يربو ربوة) when it grows. What is meant by the "surpassing seizure" is a severe punishment. It is also said: a punishment exceeding all other punishments; and it is said: one that breaks the norm.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَى الْماءُ حَمَلْناكُمْ فِي الْجارِيَةِ — Indeed when the water overflowed, We carried you in the floating [ark], (11). This is an allusion to the flood of Noah, and the "floating" (الجارية) is the ship. Those addressed are considered as having been carried in the ship of Noah, though those actually carried were their ancestors, because they all constitute a single species, and the state of some is attributed to the whole. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ — that We may make it a reminder for you, and that retentive ears may retain it. (12). This is the rationale for carrying them in the ship. Thus, the pronoun in "that We may make it" (لِنَجْعَلَها) refers to the act of carrying, considering it His act—meaning: We performed that act for you so that We might make it a matter by which you remember, a lesson by which you take heed, and an exhortation by which you are admonished.

His saying: "and that retentive ears may retain it" (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). "Retaining" (الوعي) is to place something in a vessel. What is meant by the ear "retaining" it is its being established in the soul and preserved therein, so that its benefit—which is remembrance and taking heed—may follow.

In the verse, with both its clauses, there is an allusion to Divine guidance (الهداية الربوبية) in both its types: guidance in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق) and guidance in the sense of "conveying to the goal" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب).

To clarify: It is part of the general Divine Sunnah (السنة الربوبية) — the established way of Allah — prevailing in the cosmos to guide every species of creation toward its appropriate perfection according to its specific existence, by equipping it with what drives it toward its end. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Ta-Ha: 50), and His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — "He who created and proportioned, and who determined and guided" (Al-A'la: 2-3). The clarification of this has already been provided in the commentary on Surah Ta-Ha, Surah Al-A'la, and others.

Man shares with other material species in having a generative perfection (استكمال تكويني) and an existential journey toward his existential perfection through Divine guidance that drives him toward his required end. He is distinguished among them by legislative perfection (الاستكمال التشريعي); for the human soul attains perfection through its voluntary actions, by the attributes and qualities that attach to it and the dispositions (الملكات) and states it assumes in the life of this world. This is the purpose of man's existence, by which he lives an eternal, felicitous life.

This is the reason necessitating the legislation of the religious way (السنة الدينية) by sending messengers and revealing books, and guiding toward them, لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ — "so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah after the apostles" (An-Nisa: 165). This was detailed in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of this book and elsewhere. This is guidance in the sense of "showing the way" and making known the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — which man cannot but traverse. The Exalted said: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً — "Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful" (Al-Insan: 3). If he adheres to the path and traverses it, he lives a good and felicitous life; if he abandons it and turns away, he perishes in eternal wretchedness, and the proof is established against him in any case. The Exalted said: لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ — "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (Al-Anfal: 42).

Once this is established, it becomes clear that it is part of the Divine Sunnah to guide people to the felicity of their lives by showing the way leading to it; this is alluded to by His saying: "that We may make it a reminder for you" (لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً). For a reminder does not necessitate remembrance from the one reminded; it may take effect or it may fail to do so.

It is also part of the Divine Sunnah to guide things to their perfections in the sense of bringing them to their conclusion and conveying them to it by moving them and driving them toward it. This is alluded to by His saying: "and that retentive ears may retain it" (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). For the mentioned "retaining" is among the instances of being guided by Divine guidance. The reason the Exalted did not attribute the "retaining" to Himself as He attributed the "reminder" to Himself is that what is required by the reminder is the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), which is from Allah. As for the "retaining," although it is attributed to Him just as it is attributed to man, the context is one of invitation and the mention of reward and recompense for answering the invitation. Reward and recompense are among the effects of "retaining" insofar as it is an act of man attributed to him, not insofar as it is attributed to Allah, the Exalted.

It appears from the noble verse that external events have an influence on the actions of man, just as it appears from verses like His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "If the people of the towns had believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened for them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (Al-A'raf: 96), that the actions of man have an influence on external events. Some discussion on this has already preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying: "that We may make it a reminder for you" (لِنَجْعَلَها لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً), he said: "For the Ummah of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. How many a ship has perished and how many a trace has vanished—meaning what remained of the ship until the Ummah of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reached it—so they saw it; its planks used to be seen on [Mount] Judi."

I say: What supports this has already preceded in the story of Noah in the commentary of Surah Hud.

In the same source, it is narrated that Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Makhul, who said: "When 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'I asked my Lord to make it the ear of Ali.'" Makhul said: "Thus, Ali used to say: 'I never heard anything from the Messenger of Allah and then forgot it.'"

In the same source, it is narrated that Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Wahidi, Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn ‘Asakir, and Ibn al-Najari recorded from Buraydah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to Ali: 'Indeed, Allah has commanded me to draw you near and not distance you, and to teach you so that you may retain, and it is right for you to retain.' Then this verse was revealed: 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ)."

In the same source, Abu Nu'aym recorded in al-Hilyah from Ali, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'O Ali, indeed Allah has commanded me to draw you near and teach you so that you may retain.' Then this verse was revealed: 'and that retentive ears may retain it' (وَتَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَةٌ). So you are the retentive ear for my knowledge."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from Sa'd ibn Abdullah with his chain of narration from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, and from al-Kulayni with his chain from him عليه‌السلام, and from Ibn Babawayh with his chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام.

It was also narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from Hilyat al-Awliya from Umar ibn Ali; and by al-Wahidi in Asbab al-Nuzul from Buraydah; and by Abu al-Qasim ibn Habib in his Tafsir from Zirr ibn Hubaysh from Ali عليه‌السلام.

In Ghayat al-Maram, sixteen hadiths have been narrated in this regard from the paths of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a], and it is stated in al-Burhan that Muhammad ibn al-Abbas narrated thirty hadiths therein from the paths of the general public (العامة) and the specific group (الخاصة).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (١٣)
When the Trumpet is blown with a single blast (13)
وَحُمِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ فَدُكَّتَا دَكَّةً وَٰحِدَةً (١٤)
and the earth and the mountains are lifted and levelled with a single levelling, (14)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١٥)
then, on that day, will the Imminent [Hour] befall (15)
وَٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَهِىَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ (١٦)
and the sky will be split open—for it will be frail on that day— (16)
وَٱلْمَلَكُ عَلَىٰٓ أَرْجَآئِهَا ۚ وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَـٰنِيَةٌ (١٧)
with the angels all over it, and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels]. (17)
يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ (١٨)
That day you will be presented [before your Lord]: none of your secrets will remain hidden. (18)
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ هَآؤُمُ ٱقْرَءُوا۟ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (١٩)
As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will say, ‘Here, take and read my book! (19)
إِنِّى ظَنَنتُ أَنِّى مُلَـٰقٍ حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٠)
Indeed I knew that I will encounter my account [of deeds].’ (20)
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ (٢١)
So he will have a pleasant life, (21)
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ (٢٢)
in an elevated garden, (22)
قُطُوفُهَا دَانِيَةٌ (٢٣)
whose clusters [of fruits] will be within easy reach. (23)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَآ أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَيَّامِ ٱلْخَالِيَةِ (٢٤)
[He will be told]: ‘Enjoy your food and drink, for what you had sent in advance in past days [for your future life].’ (24)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِشِمَالِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ (٢٥)
But as for him who is given his book in his left hand, he will say, ‘I wish I had not been given my book, (25)
وَلَمْ أَدْرِ مَا حِسَابِيَهْ (٢٦)
nor had I ever known what my account is! (26)
يَـٰلَيْتَهَا كَانَتِ ٱلْقَاضِيَةَ (٢٧)
I wish death had been the end of it all! (27)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنِّى مَالِيَهْ ۜ (٢٨)
My wealth did not avail me. (28)
هَلَكَ عَنِّى سُلْطَـٰنِيَهْ (٢٩)
My authority has left me.’ (29)
خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ (٣٠)
[The angels will be told:] ‘Seize him, and fetter him! (30)
ثُمَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ (٣١)
Then put him into hell. (31)
ثُمَّ فِى سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَٱسْلُكُوهُ (٣٢)
Then bind him in a chain, seventy cubits in length. (32)
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٣٣)
Indeed he had no faith in Allah, the All-supreme, (33)
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (٣٤)
and he did not urge the feeding of the needy, (34)
فَلَيْسَ لَهُ ٱلْيَوْمَ هَـٰهُنَا حَمِيمٌ (٣٥)
so he has no friend here today, (35)
وَلَا طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ (٣٦)
nor any food except pus, (36)
لَّا يَأْكُلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْخَـٰطِـُٔونَ (٣٧)
which no one shall eat except the iniquitous.’ (37)

(Commentary)

This is the second section of the verses, defining the Inevitable (الحاقة) — the reality of the Resurrection that must surely come to pass — by some of its signs and a portion of what occurs therein.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ واحِدَةٌ "When the Trumpet is blown with a single blast" (13). It has been previously mentioned that the blowing of the Trumpet (الصور) is an allusion to the Resurrection and the summoning for the final judgment. Describing the blast as "single" (واحدة) points to the swift execution of the command and the efficacy of Divine Power (القدرة); there is no weakness in it such that it would require a repetition of the blast. What immediately comes to mind from the context of the verses is that this is the second blast, which brings the dead back to life.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً "and the earth and the mountains are lifted and leveled with a single leveling" (14). Crushing (الدك) is the most intense form of pounding, which is to break a thing and transform it into small fragments. The "lifting" (حمل) of the earth and mountains refers to the encompassment of Divine Power over them. Describing the leveling as "single" indicates the speed of their disintegration, such that it does not require a second leveling.

His saying, the Exalted: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ "On that day the Imminent Event will come to pass" (15). Meaning, the Resurrection will be established.

His saying, the Exalted: وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ "and the sky will be split open, for it will be frail on that day" (16). The splitting (انشقاق) of a thing is the separation of one part of it from another. Frail (واهية) is derived from al-wahy (الوهي), meaning weakness. It is also said to be from al-wahy in the sense of a tear in leather, a garment, or the like.

It is possible that this verse—meaning His saying: وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها—carries the same meaning as His saying: وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ بِالْغَمامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلائِكَةُ تَنْزِيلاً "The day when the sky will be split open with the clouds and the angels will be sent down in a [majestic] descent" (25:25).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ "with the angels all over its sides, and on that day eight [angels] will bear the Throne of your Lord above them" (17). Al-Raghib said: "The raja (رجا) of a well or the sky or anything else is its side, and the plural is arja’ (أرجاء). The Exalted said: وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى أَرْجائِها." End quote. The term "angel" (الملك) — as has been said — can refer to a single angel or a collective, and what is intended in the verse is the collective.

Regarding His saying: وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ, the pronoun in "above them" (فَوْقَهُمْ) refers to the angels, according to the apparent requirement of the context. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the created beings (الخلائق).

The apparent sense of His speech is that the Throne (العرش) — the locus of Divine command and authority — has bearers from among the angels today, as the Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful" (40:7). Narrations have stated that they are four. The apparent sense of the verse—meaning His saying: وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ—is that the bearers on the Day of Resurrection will be eight. Whether they are from the angels or from others, the verse is silent on that, although the context is not devoid of an indication that they are from the angels.

It is possible—as previously hinted—that the purpose of mentioning the splitting of the sky, the angels being on its sides, and the bearers of the Throne being eight on that day, is to describe the manifestation of the angels, the sky, and the Throne to man on that day. The Exalted said: وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord" (39:75).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لا تَخْفى مِنْكُمْ خافِيَةٌ "On that day you will be exposed: none of your secrets will remain hidden" (18). It is apparent that what is intended is the presentation (العرض) before Allah, as the Exalted said: وَعُرِضُوا عَلى رَبِّكَ صَفًّا "and they shall be presented before your Lord in ranks" (18:48). Presentation (العرض) is like a seller showing his goods to a buyer by spreading them out before him. Thus, the presentation on that day before Allah—which is the Day of Judgment—is the bringing forth of what man possesses of belief and action in a manner that no hidden belief or hidden deed remains concealed. This occurs by the transformation of the Unseen (الغيب) into the witnessed (شهادة) and the secret into the manifest. He said: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ "on the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9), and He said: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ "the day when they will emerge [from their graves]: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" (40:16).

It has been established in our previous discussions that those things counted in the speech of the Exalted as characteristics of the Day of Resurrection—such as the exclusivity of sovereignty to Allah, the command belonging to Him, the absence of any protector from Him, the emergence of creation before Him, and the fact that nothing of them is hidden from Him—all of these are eternally established for the Exalted. What is intended is the manifestation of these realities on that day with a clarity that leaves no veil or doubt.

The meaning, then, is: On that day it will become manifest that you are in a state of presentation before the knowledge of Allah, and every hidden deed among your actions will be revealed.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَيَقُولُ هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ "As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will say, ‘Here, take and read my book!" (19). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "هاؤم is a command for a group, equivalent to hakum (هاكم). You say to one: ha’a (هاء) O man; to two: ha’uma (هاؤما); to a group: ha’um (هاؤم); to a woman: ha’i (هاء) with a kasrah on the hamzah and no ya’ after it; to two women: ha’uma; and to a group of women: ha’unna. This is the dialect of the people of Hijaz.

The tribes of Tamim and Qays say: ha’a (هاء) for a man like the people of Hijaz; for two: ha’a (هاءا); for a group: ha’u (هاؤا); for a woman: ha’i (هائي); and for women: ha’unna.

Some Arabs place a kaf in place of the hamzah, saying: hak, hakuma, hakum, haki, hakuma, hakunna. Its meaning is: 'Take' or 'receive'. It is used as a command and not as a prohibition." End quote.

This verse and those following it until His saying: الْخاطِؤُنَ (the sinners) provide a detailed explanation of the differing states of people on that day in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء). A discussion on the meaning of being given the book in the right hand has already passed in the commentary of His saying: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ (17:71). It is apparent that His saying: هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ is an address to the angels. The ha’ in كِتابِيَهْ, as well as at the ends of the subsequent verses, is for the purpose of pausing (الوقف) and is called the ha’ of rest (هاء الاستراحة).

The meaning is: As for him who is given his book in his right hand, he says to the angels: "Take and read my book," meaning it is a book that decrees my felicity.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي ظَنَنْتُ أَنِّي مُلاقٍ حِسابِيَهْ "Indeed I knew that I will encounter my account" (20). Here, "thought" (الظن) carries the meaning of certainty (اليقين). The verse serves as a justification for what is gained from the previous verse. The essence of the justification is: My book was only the book of the right hand, decreeing my felicity, because I was certain in the world that I would encounter my account; thus, I believed in my Lord and rectified my deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ "So he will be in a pleasant life" (21). Meaning, he lives a life that he is pleased with. Attributing "pleasure" (الرضا) to the "life" (العيشة) is a form of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي).

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ "in a lofty garden" (22) — until His saying — الْخالِيَةِ "bygone [days]" (24). Meaning, he is in a garden lofty in status, containing that which no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has never occurred to the heart of any human.

His saying: قُطُوفُها دانِيَةٌ "with its clusters [of fruits] within reach" (23). Qutuf (القطوف) is the plural of qitf (قطف), which refers to the fruit that is harvested. The meaning is: its fruits are near to him, and he may take them however he wishes.

His saying: كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِي الْأَيَّامِ الْخالِيَةِ "‘Eat and drink healthfully for what you had sent ahead in bygone days’" (24). Meaning, it is said to them: Eat and drink from all that is eaten and drunk therein, in a state of being healthful (هنيئا) for you, because of the faith and righteous deeds you sent forward in the world whose days have passed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ فَيَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُوتَ كِتابِيَهْ وَلَمْ أَدْرِ ما حِسابِيَهْ "But as for him who is given his book in his left hand, he will say, ‘I wish I had not been given my book, and had not known what my account is!" (25-26). These are the second group, the wretched criminals. They are given the record of their deeds in their left hand. The discussion on its meaning has passed in Surah al-Isra’. These people wish they had not been given their book nor known their account; they wish this because of the painful punishment they witness prepared for them.

His saying, the Exalted: يا لَيْتَها كانَتِ الْقاضِيَةَ "I wish it had been the end!" (27). They have mentioned that the pronoun in "it" (لَيْتَها) refers to the first death that man tasted in the world.

The meaning is: I wish the first death I tasted had been the final end (القاضية) for me, decreeing my non-existence, so that I would have perished and not been resurrected alive to fall into the abyss of eternal punishment and witness what I witness.

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَغْنى عَنِّي مالِيَهْ هَلَكَ عَنِّي سُلْطانِيَهْ "My wealth has not availed me. My authority has departed from me" (28-29). These are two expressions of intense regret (تحسر) that he utters when he sees the failure of his efforts in the world. He used to think that the key to his happiness in life was wealth and authority, which would repel every dislikeable thing from him and give him power over everything he loved and desired. Thus, he exerted all his effort in acquiring them, turned away from his Lord and from every truth to which he was called, and denied his caller. When he witnesses the severing of all means and that he is in a day when neither wealth nor sons avail, he mentions the uselessness of his wealth and the vanishing of his authority in regret and pain. But what use is regret?

His saying, the Exalted: خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ "‘Seize him and fetter him" (30) — until His saying — فَاسْلُكُوهُ "and then chain him" (32). This is a narration of the command of the Exalted to the angels to seize him and enter him into the Fire. The implied meaning is: It is said to the angels, "Seize him," etc. فَغُلُّوهُ is a command from al-ghull (الغل), which is to bind with a shackle (غل) that joins the hand, the foot, and the neck.

His saying: ثُمَّ الْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ "then put him into hell" (31). Meaning, enter him into the Great Fire and make him endure it.

His saying: ثُمَّ فِي سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُها سَبْعُونَ ذِراعاً فَاسْلُكُوهُ "then chain him in a chain whose length is seventy cubits" (32). The chain (السلسلة) is the fetter. The dhar‘ (الذرع) is the length. The cubit (الذراع) is the distance between the elbow and the tip of the fingers, which is a unit of length. To "chain him" (سلوكه فيه) means to place him within it. The sum of the meaning is: Then place him in a fetter whose length is seventy cubits.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ الْعَظِيمِ وَلا يَحُضُّ عَلى طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ "Indeed he had no faith in Allah, the All-supreme, and he did not urge the feeding of the needy" (33-34). Urging (الحض) is to incite and encourage. These two verses serve as the justification for the command to seize and enter him into the Fire. That is, the seizing, then the burning in Hell, and the chaining in the chain are because he did not believe in Allah, the All-supreme, and did not urge the feeding of the needy; meaning, he was negligent regarding the affairs of the poor and indifferent to what they suffered.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَيْسَ لَهُ الْيَوْمَ هاهُنا حَمِيمٌ "So today he has no loyal friend here" (35) — until His saying — الْخاطِؤُنَ "the sinners" (37). The loyal friend (الحميم) is the close friend. The verse is a corollary to His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ etc. The sum of the meaning is: Since he did not believe in Allah, the All-supreme, he has no friend here today who can benefit him—meaning an intercessor (شفيع) to intercede for him—for there is no forgiveness for a disbeliever, and thus no intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah.

His saying: وَلا طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ "nor any food except pus" (36). Ghislin (الغسلين) is the wash-water; what is intended is that which flows from the bodies of the people of the Fire, such as pus and the like. The verse is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: حَمِيمٌ, and is a corollary to His saying: وَلا يَحُضُ etc. The sum of the meaning is: Since he did not urge the feeding of the needy, he has no food here today except from the ghislin of the people of the Fire.

His saying: لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ "which none but the sinners eat" (37). This is a description of the ghislin. The sinners (الخاطؤُنَ) are those who are steeped in error and sin.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Zayd that he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: يحمله اليوم أربعة ويوم القيامة ثمانية — "Four bear it today, and on the Day of Resurrection, eight."

I say: In the restriction of the bearers in the verse by His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ (on that day), there is an indication—rather, a clear manifestation—that this specific number is exclusive to the Resurrection.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in another tradition, it is said: "Eight bear it: four from the former ones and four from the latter ones. As for the four from the former ones, they are Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. As for the four from the latter ones, they are Muhammad, Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn عليه‌السلام."

I say: In more than one narration, it is stated that the eight are specific to the Day of Resurrection. In some of them, it is said that the bearers of the Throne—and the Throne is knowledge (العلم)—are "four from us and four from whomsoever Allah wills."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: "When it is the Day of Resurrection, every group of people will be called by their Imam—the one who died in their era. If he affirms him, he will be given his book in his right hand, because of His saying: يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ (17:71). So whoever is given his book in his right hand, those are the ones who read their book. The 'right hand' (اليمين) is the affirmation of the Imam, for he is his book which he reads..." until he said: "And whoever denied will be among the companions of the left—those of whom Allah said: وَأَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ما أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ـ فِي سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ وَظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ (56:41-43) etc."

I say: In several narrations, the application of His saying: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ etc., is to Ali عليه‌السلام, and in some, to him and his followers (شيعته). Likewise, the application of His saying: وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ etc., is to his enemies. These are instances of al-jary (الجري) — the application of a verse to its subsequent instances — rather than primary exegesis.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: لو أن دلوا من غسلين يهراق في الدنيا لأنتن بأهل الدنيا — "If a bucket of ghislin were poured into the world, it would cause the people of the world to rot from its stench."

In the same source, al-Bayhaqi narrated in Shu‘ab al-Iman from Sa‘sa‘ah ibn Sawhan, who said: A Bedouin came to Ali ibn Abi Talib and said: "How is this word [read]: لا يأكله إلا الخاطون (those who walk)? By Allah, everyone walks (yakhtu)." Ali smiled and said: "O Bedouin, it is لا يَأْكُلُهُ إِلَّا الْخاطِؤُنَ (none but the sinners eat it)." He said: "You have spoken the truth, by Allah, O Commander of the Faithful—Allah would not abandon His servant."

Then Ali turned to Abu al-Aswad and said: "The non-Arabs (الأعاجم) have entered the religion in large numbers, so set down something for the people by which they may be guided to the correctness of their tongues." So he established for them the raf‘, the nasb, and the khafd (the grammatical cases).

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babuwayh in al-Duru‘ al-Waqiyah, in a tradition from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: ولو أن ذراعا من السلسلة التي ذكرها الله في كتابه ـ وضع على جميع جبال الدنيا لذابت عن حرها — "If a single cubit of the chain that Allah mentioned in His Book were placed upon all the mountains of the world, they would melt from its heat."

Ayahs

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٨)
I swear by what you see (38)
وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٩)
and what you do not see: (39)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (٤٠)
it is indeed the speech of a noble apostle (40)
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ (٤١)
and it is not the speech of a poet. Little is the faith that you have! (41)
وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٢)
Nor is it the speech of a soothsayer. Little is the admonition that you take! (42)
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٣)
Gradually sent down from the Lord of all the worlds. (43)
وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ (٤٤)
Had he faked any sayings in Our name, (44)
لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٤٥)
We would have surely seized him by the right hand (45)
ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ ٱلْوَتِينَ (٤٦)
and then cut off his aorta, (46)
فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَـٰجِزِينَ (٤٧)
and none of you could have held Us off from him. (47)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٨)
Indeed it is a reminder for the Godwary. (48)
وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنكُم مُّكَذِّبِينَ (٤٩)
Indeed We know that there are some among you who deny [it]. (49)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٠)
And indeed it will be a [matter of] regret for the faithless. (50)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٥١)
It is indeed certain truth. (51)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٥٢)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (52)

(Commentary)

This is the third section of the verses of the Surah, reinforcing what preceded regarding the matter of the Inevitable Hour (الحاقة) through the tongue of confirming the Noble Quran, thereby establishing the truth of what it has foretold concerning the Resurrection (القيامة).

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ "I swear by what you see, and what you do not see!" (69:38-39). The apparent meaning of the verse is an oath by what is witnessed by them and what they do not witness—that is, the world of the seen (الشهادة) and the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge. Thus, it is an oath by the totality of creation. It does not include His Transcendent Essence, for it is far removed from the etiquette of the Quran to join the Creator and the creation in a single rank, or for Him, the Exalted, to glorify Himself and His handiwork with a shared glorification in the same breath.

In an oath, there is a type of glorification and veneration for the object of the oath. His creation, the Exalted, insofar as it is His creation, is majestic and beautiful, because He, the Exalted, is Beautiful and nothing proceeds from Him except the beautiful. Indeed, He, the Exalted, has deemed His own act good and praised Himself for His creation in His saying: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ "who perfected everything He created" (32:7), and His saying: فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ "Blessed is Allah, the best of creators" (23:14). Thus, existent beings possess nothing from Him, the Exalted, except beauty; whatever ugliness exists beyond that stems from their own selves or from the comparison of one to another.

In choosing "what they see and what they do not see" as the object of the oath to prove the truth of the Quran, there is an undeniable suitability. For the single, interconnected system (النظام) whose parts are intertwined and which runs through the totality of the universe necessitates His Oneness, the Exalted, and the return of all things to Him. This, in turn, necessitates the sending of messengers and the revelation of books, and the Quran is the best of heavenly books, guiding to the truth in all of that and to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah.

From what has preceded, the lack of soundness in the view that "what you see and what you do not see" refers to the creation and the Creator becomes clear, as the context does not support it. Similarly, the views that it refers to manifest and hidden blessings, or to the jinn, humans, and angels, or to bodies and souls, or to this world and the Hereafter, or to the observed effects of power and its unobserved secrets, are also weak; for the term is broader in its denotation (المدلول) than all of those.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ "it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger" (69:40). The pronoun refers to the Quran. What is understood from the context is that the "noble messenger" refers to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. This is a confirmation of his messengership (الرسالة) in the face of their claims that he was a poet or a soothsayer.

There is no harm in attributing the Quran to his speech, for it is only attributed to him insofar as he is a messenger. A messenger (الرسول), qua messenger, brings nothing but the speech of the One who sent him. This has been clarified with the utmost clarity in His subsequent saying: تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ "a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (69:43).

It has been said that the "noble messenger" refers to Gabriel, but the context does not support this. Had he been intended, it would have been more appropriate to negate its being something brought down by devils, as was done in Surah al-Shu‘ara’. Furthermore, His subsequent saying: وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ الْأَقَاوِيلِ "Had he faked any sayings in Our name" (69:44) and what follows it, is only suitable if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم is the one intended by the "noble messenger."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ "and it is not the speech of a poet; little is it that you believe" (69:41). This is a negation that the Quran is a composed verse authored by a poet; the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم never composed poetry nor was he a poet.

His saying: قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ "little is it that you believe" is a reproach to their society, as the majority of them did not believe, and none believed except a few.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ "nor is it the speech of a soothsayer; little is it that you take admonition" (69:42). This is a negation that the Quran is soothsaying (الكهانة) or that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was a soothsayer who received the Quran from the jinn who cast it to him.

His saying: قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ "little is it that you take admonition" is also a reproach to their society.

His saying, the Exalted: تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ "a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (69:43). That is, it is sent down from the Lord of all the worlds and is not the fabrication of the Messenger who attributed it to Allah, as previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ الْأَقَاوِيلِ — until His saying — حَاجِزِينَ. It is said: تقول (he fabricated) against so-and-so when he invents a saying from himself and attributes it to him. The وتين (aorta) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is a vein that supplies the liver, and if it is severed, its owner dies; it is also said to be the life-line of the heart.

The meaning is: "Had he fabricated against Us" — this noble messenger whom We charged with Our message and sent to you with a Quran We revealed to him — and had he invented "some sayings" and attributed them to Us, "We would have surely seized him by the right hand" (لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ), just as a criminal is seized by his hand. Or it means We would have severed his right hand, or it means We would have taken vengeance upon him with power (القوة), as in the narration of al-Qummi. "Then We would have surely cut off his aorta" (ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ) and killed him for fabricating against Us. "And none of you could have" (فَمَا مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ) "shielded him" (حَاجِزِينَ) — that is, protected him from Us or saved him from Our punishment and destruction.

This is a threat to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم on the assumption that he were to forge a lie against Allah and attribute to Him something He did not say, while he is a messenger from Him whom He honored with His prophethood and chose for His message.

Thus, the verses are similar in meaning to His saying: وَلَوْلَا أَنْ ثَبَّتْنَاكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئًا قَلِيلًا إِذًا لَأَذَقْنَاكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَيَاةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَمَاتِ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly inclined toward them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us" (17:74-75). Likewise is His saying regarding the prophets after mentioning His great blessings upon them: وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "and had they ascribed any partners [to Him], what they used to do would have surely come to naught" (6:88).

Therefore, the objection does not hold that the verses imply that anyone who claims prophethood and forges lies against Allah will be destroyed and punished by Allah in this world with the severest punishment—a claim refuted by the existence of some lying claimants to prophethood.

This is because the threat in the verse is directed at the Messenger who is truthful in his message, should he fabricate against Allah and attribute to Him some things that are not from Him; it is not directed at every claimant to prophethood who is a fabricator against Allah in his very claim of prophethood and his reporting from Allah the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "Indeed it is a reminder for the God-conscious" (69:48). It reminds them, by virtue of their godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure, of the realities of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and informs them of their ranks with Allah and their stations in the Hereafter and Paradise. That which possesses such a quality cannot be a fabrication or a forgery. Thus, the verse is presented as an argument for the Quran being free from fabrication and forgery.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنْكُمْ مُكَذِّبِينَ وَإِنَّهُ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ "Indeed We know that there are some among you who deny [it], and indeed it will be a [cause of] regret for the faithless" (69:49-50). This will become manifest to them on the Day of Regret.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَحَقُّ الْيَقِينِ فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ "Indeed it is the certain truth. So celebrate the name of your Lord, the All-supreme" (69:51-52). Discussion has already preceded regarding the counterparts of these two verses at the end of Surah al-Waqi‘ah. The two Surahs are united in their objective, which is the description of the Day of Resurrection, and they are united in the context of their conclusions, which is the oath upon the reality of the Quran that foretells the Day of Resurrection. Both Surahs conclude with the fact that the Quran and what it foretells regarding the occurrence of the Inevitable Event is the certain truth (حق اليقين). This is followed by the command to glorify the Name of the Supreme Lord, who is transcendent above creating the world in vain without a Return, and above the nullification of the true knowledge that the Quran provides regarding the matter of the Origin and the Return.

— Completed, and praise be to Allah —
